text: March 29, 2019 (this “Amendment”) and amends that certain the Distributor Motor Fuel Agreement dated as of January 23, 2018 (the “Agreement”), by and between Sunoco, LLC ("Company"), and 7-Eleven, Inc. and SEI Fuel Services, Inc. (collectively, "Distributor"). Background: A. The Parties wish to enter into this Amendment to amend and modify the Agreement, as more particularly provided herein. NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the parties hereto agree to amend the Agreement as follows: 1. Waiver of Make-Up Payment. The Parties have agreed that any Make-Up Payment otherwise due from Distributor to Company for the first Contract Year shall be waived. The difference between the Expected Annual Supply Margin for the first Contract Year and the actual Annual Supply Margin attained for the first Contract Year shall be added (as, and not in addition to, the Shortfall amount) to the Expected Annual Supply Margin for the second Contract Year as of April 1, 2019. The Parties agree to work together to find the means to ramp up growth volume in the second Contract Year to a level sufficient to avoid having Make-Up Payments in the future. 2. Bulk Sales of Fuel. The Parties agree that to help Distributor satisfy its Expected Annual Supply Margin obligations pursuant to this Agreement, Distributor may elect to purchase motor fuel in bulk from Company at Company’s cost plus [***] (the “Bulk Volume”). Bulk Volume purchased during this period shall be applied to Distributor’s Expected Annual Supply Margin obligations for the Contract Year in which the purchases occur. Volumes, locations, and timing requested of Bulk Volume purchases by Distributor will be evaluated by Company and approved in Company’s commercially reasonable discretion. Upon the agreement by Company and Distributor with respect to timing, location and volumes of Bulk Volume purchases, Distributor commits to purchasing the Bulk Volume that has been nominated. Company’s general terms and conditions applicable to bulk fuel sales will apply for all purchases of Bulk Volume hereunder unless the Parties agree otherwise in writing. 3. Expected Annual Supply Margins. Section 1.6(b) of the Agreement is hereby amended to provide that (a) for the second Contract Year, the Expected Annual Supply Margin shall [***], (b) for the third Contract Year and for each Contract Year thereafter, the Expected Annual Supply Margin shall be [***]. 4. Additional Locations. Section 1.9(b) of the Agreement is hereby amended to add the followings sentences at the end of such section: “Notwithstanding the foregoing, Distributor may add an unlimited number of additional Locations (the “Additional Locations”) with the consent of Company, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed and may only be withheld if (A) such Additional Location will create a potential conflict with any other existing Company supplied retail gasoline station, or (B) Company is unable to supply such proposed Additional Location on a commercially reasonable basis.

text: This Management Services Agreement (the “Agreement”) is dated as of June 1, 2019 (the “Effective Date”) and is between Steel Services Ltd. (“Steel Services”), a Delaware corporation, having an office at 590 Madison Avenue, 32nd Floor, New York, New York 10022 and Steel Connect, Inc., a Delaware corporation (the “Company”), having an office at 1601 Trapelo Road, Suite 170, Waltham, Massachusetts 02451. WHEREAS, the Company desires to have Steel Services furnish certain services to the Company, as set forth on Exhibit A attached hereto, as it may be amended from time to time pursuant to the terms hereof (the “Services”), and Steel Services has agreed to furnish the Services, pursuant to the terms and conditions hereinafter set forth. The Company has obtained all necessary approvals under its corporate governance documents for its entry into this Agreement. WHEREAS, a Special Committee (the “Committee”) of the Board of Directors of the Company (the “Board”) comprised of disinterested directors approved this Agreement and recommended the Board’s approval, and a majority of the disinterested directors of the Company has voted to approve this Agreement. NOW, THEREFORE, the parties therefore agree as follows: Section 1.Engagement of Steel Services 1.01. During the term of this Agreement, Steel Services shall provide to the Company the Services by way of the non-exclusive services of persons designated by Steel Services to perform the Services in accordance with the terms and provisions of this Agreement (the “Designated Persons”), as described and defined on Exhibit A, as may be necessary or desirable or as may be reasonably requested or required in connection with the business, operations, and affairs (both ordinary and extraordinary) of the Company and its subsidiaries and affiliates. 1.02. In performing the Services, Steel Services shall be subject to the supervision and control of the Committee and shall report to the Committee and/or such other person designated by the Committee (an “Authorized Person”) in accordance with such procedures as may be adopted by the Committee and Steel Services from time to time. Steel Services shall provide a quarterly report to the Committee or the Authorized Person summarizing the Services provided in such detail as the Committee or such Authorized Person and Steel Services shall mutually agree (each a “Quarterly Report”). Each Quarterly Report shall be due on or before the 60th day following the expiration of the applicable fiscal quarter of the Company. Steel Services may 1 incur obligations or enter into transactions on behalf of the Company subject to any mutually agreed upon limits as established from time to time by the Committee and Steel Services.

text: EXECUTION VERSION TRANSITION SERVICES AGREEMENT This Transition Services Agreement (this “Agreement”) is made and entered into this 26th day of August, 2019, by and among Kaman Corporation, a Connecticut corporation (“Seller”), LJ KIT Blocker, Inc., a Delaware corporation (“Purchaser 1”), LJ KAI Blocker, Inc., a Delaware corporation (“Purchaser 2”), and LJ KFP Blocker, Inc., a Delaware corporation (collectively with Purchaser 1 and Purchaser 2, “Purchasers,” and each individually, a “Purchaser”) and Kaman Industrial Technologies Corporation, a Connecticut corporation (the “Company”). WITNESSETH: WHEREAS, Seller and Purchasers have entered into a Share Purchase Agreement, dated as of June 25, 2019 (the “Purchase Agreement”), pursuant to which, at the Closing, Seller has agreed to sell and transfer the Shares of the Company to Purchasers, and Purchasers have agreed to purchase the Shares of the Company from Seller; WHEREAS, Seller currently provides certain services to the Company and its subsidiaries, including (i) Ruby Automation, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company (as the successor company to Kaman Automation, Inc.), (ii) Kaman Fluid Power, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, and (iii) Ruby PR, LLC, a Puerto Rico limited liability company (as the successor company to Industrial Rubber & Mechanics, Inc.) (collectively, the “Subsidiaries” and each a “Subsidiary”); WHEREAS, in order to assist in the transition of the Business to Purchasers after the Closing, upon the terms and subject to the conditions set forth in this Agreement, Purchasers desire to have the Company and its Subsidiaries receive from Seller, and Seller has agreed to provide, or cause to be provided, certain transition services as set forth herein to the Company and its Subsidiaries; and WHEREAS, Purchasers and the Company acknowledge that Seller is not in the business of providing the transition services set forth in this Agreement to third parties and that such services will be provided by Seller at Purchasers’ request and solely to accommodate Purchasers and the Company and its Subsidiaries during the transitional period after the Closing so as to maintain the continuity of the Business and to enable the Company and its Subsidiaries to establish stand-alone status without continued reliance upon the services of Seller (“Transition”). NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing and the mutual representations, warranties, covenants and agreements set forth herein, and upon the terms and subject to the conditions set forth in this Agreement, the Parties hereto agree as follows: ARTICLE I DEFINITIONS Capitalized terms used but not defined in this Agreement will have the respective meanings ascribed to such terms in the Purchase Agreement, except as expressly provided herein. ARTICLE II SERVICES Section 2.1Scope of Services.

text: This Transition Services Agreement (this “Agreement”), dated as of November 1, 2019 (the “Effective Date”), is made by and between Aytu Bioscience, Inc., a Delaware corporation (“Buyer”) and Cerecor, Inc., a Delaware corporation (“Seller”). All capitalized terms used herein (or in any schedule) but not defined herein have the meanings given to them in the Purchase Agreement (defined below). Recitals A. Buyer and Seller are parties to an Asset Purchase Agreement, dated as of October 10, 2019, (the “Purchase Agreement”), under which Buyer agreed to purchase from Seller certain assets of Seller used in the operation of the Business. B. In connection with the Purchase Agreement and transactions contemplated thereby, and for purposes of easing the transition of the Business to Buyer and minimizing interruptions to the continuing operations of Seller, the parties have agreed to enter into this Agreement for the purpose of providing certain transition services to each other. Agreement In consideration of the mutual covenants contained herein, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, Buyer and Seller agree: 1. Transition Services. (a) Applicable Transition Services. Upon the terms and subject to the conditions contained herein and in the Schedules hereto, Seller shall provide, or cause its Affiliates to provide, to Buyer the services described on Schedule A-1 and Schedule A-3 (together with the services described in the other provisions of this Agreement that Seller is obligated to provide to Buyer, collectively, the “Seller-Provided Services”) and Buyer shall provide, or cause its Affiliates to provide, to Seller the services described on Schedule A-2 and Schedule A-3 (together with the services described in the other provisions of this Agreement that Buyer is obligated to provide to Seller collectively, “Buyer-Provided Services,” and together with the Seller-Provided Services, the “Transition Services,” it being understood that the services described on Schedule A-3 are mutual services that each party will provide to the other). The parties agree to negotiate in good faith the provision of any additional related services reasonably requested by Buyer to be provided by Seller, or by Seller to be provided by Buyer, including any applicable pricing. Any such additional services so provided by Seller or Buyer, as applicable, shall constitute Transition Services under this Agreement and be subject to this Agreement as if fully set forth on Schedule A-1, Schedule A-2, or Schedule A-3, as applicable, beginning as of the commencement of such additional services. During the first three (3) months following Closing, Buyer will provide the Transition Services free of charge. Thereafter, Seller will pay Buyer for any continuing Buyer-Provided Services related to the continued commercialization of the Excluded Products at the rate specified in Schedule A-2. The Transition Services provided by Sellers will be free of charge throughout the term of this agreement. (b) Non-Assignable Assets.

(“Purchaser”) and Vital Therapies, Inc. (“Seller”). The words and terms defined in the Asset Purchase Agreement (defined below) shall have the same meaning when used in this First Amendment unless the context expressly requires otherwise RECITALS A.Whereas, on March 13, 2019 Seller and Purchaser entered into an Asset Purchase Agreement (the “Asset Purchase Agreement”) in connection with a purchase and sale of certain ELAD System related assets, or the Purchased Assets. B.Whereas, Purchaser has requested additional time in order to fund the balance of the Escrow Amount. AGREEMENT NOW THEREFORE, in consideration of and in reliance upon the covenants herein contained the parties agree as follows: 1.Extension of Escrow Funding Period. Section 2.03 of the Asset Purchase Agreement is hereby amended to change the “March 26, 2019” date to “April 2, 2019” for Purchaser to wire transfer to the Escrow Agent the remaining Escrow Amount ($1,350,000), for deposit into the Escrow Account. 2.Other Terms of the Asset Purchase Agreement. Except as amended herein, all other terms, covenants and conditions of Asset Purchase Agreement, shall remain in full force and effect. 3.Counterparts. This First Amendment may be executed in two or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument. Delivery of an executed counterpart of a signature page to this First Amendment by facsimile, portable document format (.pdf), DocuSign or other electronic transmission shall be equally as effective as delivery of a manually executed counterpart of this First Amendment. *** Signature Page Follows *** IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties hereto have executed this First Amendment as of the Effective Date. SELLER Vital Therapies, Inc. _____________________________________________ By: Duane Nash Title: President and Chief Executive Officer PURCHASER RH Cell Therapeutics Corp. _____________________________________________ By: Haibin Ruan Title: President and Chief Executive Officer SECOND AMENDMENT TO ASSET PURCHASE AGREEMENT THIS SECOND AMENDMENT TO ASSET PURCHASE AGREEMENT (the “Second Amendment”) is made as of April 4, 2019 (the “Effective Date”), by and between RH Cell Therapeutics Corp. (“Purchaser”) and Vital Therapies, Inc. (“Seller”). RECITALS A. The Buyer, the Seller and the Stockholders have previously entered in that certain Asset Purchase Agreement, dated January 18, 2019 (the “Asset Purchase Agreement”). B. The parties hereto desire to amend the Asset Purchase Agreement as set forth herein. C. Pursuant to Section 7.2 of the Asset Purchase Agreement, the Asset Purchase Agreement may be amended by the Parties only by an instrument in writing signed on behalf of the Buyer, the Seller and the Stockholders. D. Holdco is a wholly-owned subsidiary of 1847 Holdings LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, and the parent company of Buyer. AGREEMENT NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual promises herein contained, the parties hereto, intending to be legally bound, hereby agree as follows: 1. Definitions. All capitalized terms used herein without definition shall have the meanings ascribed to them in the Asset Purchase Agreement. a. Section 1.4(a) as set forth in the Asset Purchase Agreement shall be amended and restated in its entirety to read as follows: “In consideration for the sale, assignment and delivery of the Purchased Assets, Buyer shall (i) pay an aggregate purchase price equal to Six Million, Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($6,200,000) (the “Purchase Price”), as the same may be adjusted pursuant to this Agreement, payable in accordance with this Section 1.4(a) on the Closing Date, (ii) assume the Assumed Liabilities and (iii) issue to each of Steve Goedeker and Michael Goedeker shares of the Common Stock of Holdco equal to 11.25% non-dilutable interest in all of the issued and outstanding common stock of Holdco as of the Closing Date (the “Holdco Shares”).” b. Section 2.3 is hereby added to the Asset Purchase Agreement as follows: “2.3 Representations and Warranties of Holdco. Holdco represent and warrant to, and agrees with, the Seller and the Stockholders as follows: - 1 - (a) Organization. Holdco is a corporation duly incorporated and in good standing under the laws of the State of Delaware. (b) Binding Obligation. Holdco has all requisite corporate power and authority to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement. All corporate acts and other proceedings required to be taken by Holdco to authorize the execution, delivery and performance by Holdco of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, have been duly and properly taken. This Agreement has been duly-executed and delivered by Holdco and constitutes the legal, valid and binding obligation of Holdco, enforceable against Holdco in accordance with its terms.

text: EXECUTION VERSION February 9, 2018 TRMT CB Lender LLC c/o Tremont Realty Advisors LLC Two Newton Place 255 Washington Street, Suite 300 Newton, MA 02458 Attention: Douglas Lanois Re: Master Repurchase Agreement, dated as of February 9, 2018 (as amended, restated, supplemented or otherwise modified and in effect from time to time, the “Master Repurchase Agreement”) by and between TRMT CB Lender LLC (“Seller”) and Citibank, N.A. (“Buyer”). Ladies and Gentlemen: Reference is hereby made to, and this fee letter (this “Fee Letter”) is hereby incorporated by reference into, the Master Repurchase Agreement. Capitalized terms used herein that are not otherwise defined herein shall have the meanings set forth in the Master Repurchase Agreement. Section 1.Definitions. The following terms shall have the meanings set forth below. “Applicable Spread” shall mean, with respect to each Purchased Asset, the “Applicable Spread” set forth below unless otherwise agreed to between Seller and Buyer on a case-by-case basis and in each case as set forth in the related Confirmation: Property Type Multi Family Asset Applicable Spread Commercial Asset Applicable Spread Hotel Asset Applicable Spread Purchase Price Debt Yield Greater than or equal to 8.5% 200 Greater than or equal to 9.5% 210 Greater than or equal to 11.0% 225 Less than 8.5% 225 Less than 9.5% 235 Less than 11.0% 250 Notwithstanding the above, with respect to each Purchased Asset purchased pursuant to a Transaction on a Purchase Date, the “Applicable Spread” may be any other amount determined by Buyer in its sole discretion; provided, that any such change in the Applicable Spread determined by Buyer on a Purchase Date with respect to a Purchased Asset shall not have any effect on the Applicable Spread for Purchased Assets already purchased on prior Purchase Dates. “Effective Purchase Price Percentage” means, with respect to each Purchased Asset, the quotient of the outstanding Purchase Price divided by the outstanding principal balance of such Purchased Asset. “Exit Fee” shall mean, with respect to the repurchase of a Purchased Asset, an amount equal to the product of 0.5% multiplied by the then outstanding Purchase Price for such Purchased Asset. “Facility Amount” shall mean $100,000,000. “Guarantor Threshold” shall mean $1,000,000.00. “Margin Amount” shall mean, with respect to any Purchased Asset on any date, an amount equal to the product of (i) the Margin Percentage on such date with respect to such Purchased Asset and (ii) the Purchase Price of such Purchased Asset on such date. “Margin Percentage” shall mean, with respect to any Purchased Asset as of any date, a percentage equal to the quotient of (i) one (1) divided by (ii) the Purchase Price Percentage for such Purchased Asset as of any such date.

Marinello (“Executive”). W I T N E S E T H : WHEREAS, the Company has previously entered into an employment agreement with the Executive, dated March 2, 2017, (the “Prior Employment Agreement”) that agreed to employ Executive as the President and Chief Executive Officer of the Company and The Hertz Corporation, a Delaware corporation and the primary operating subsidiary of the Company (“Hertz”), and for Executive to serve as a member of the Boards of Directors of the Company and Hertz (the “Boards”); WHEREAS, the Prior Employment Agreement is scheduled to expire on December 31, 2019; WHEREAS, the Company and Executive desire to replace and supersede the Prior Employment Agreement in its entirety by entering into this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement setting forth the terms of Executive’s employment with the Company and its subsidiaries and affiliates from and after the Effective Date; NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing, the premises and mutual covenants contained in this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are acknowledged, the Company and Executive agree as follows: 1.Agreement to Employ; Employment Period; No Conflict. (a) Upon the terms and subject to the conditions of this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement, the Company agrees to employ Executive, and Executive accepts such employment, for the period commencing on the Effective Date and ending on December 31, 2020 (or such earlier date upon which Executive’s employment is terminated in accordance with Section 5). The period during which Executive is employed pursuant to this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement shall be referred to as the “Employment Period.” Concurrent with the execution of this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement, Executive shall execute the waiver and release set forth on Exhibit A attached hereto (the “Exhibit A Waiver and Release”). In consideration for Executive executing and not revoking the Exhibit A Waiver and Release, the Company hereby represents that as of the date hereof the Boards do not presently have any actual knowledge of any valid claims by the Company or Hertz against Executive. (b) Executive represents that she is entering into this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement voluntarily and that she is not subject to any contractual restriction that would prevent her from functioning as President and Chief Executive Officer of the Company and Hertz, or limit her ability to do so at any time during the Employment Period (the “Executive Representation”). (c) The Company represents that it has full authority and all necessary approvals to enter into this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement. 2.Position and Responsibilities; Location; Standard of Services. (a) Position and Responsibilities.

Mold-Masters (2007) Limited - Employment Agreement Page 1 2.3 The Employee agrees and undertakes to use the Employee's best efforts and to devote such attention, energy, skill and time as is necessary to satisfactorily fulfill all of the duties and responsibilities of the President, Mold-Masters Asia and to fulfill all duties and responsibilities assigned to the Employee by the Company from time to time. text: This Cerner Executive Severance Agreement (this “Executive Severance Agreement”), effective as of December 14, 2017 (the “Effective Date”), is a supplement to and amendment of the employment agreement dated July 14, 2003 between John T. Peterzalek (“you”/“your”) and Cerner Corporation, a Delaware corporation (“Cerner”). RECITALS A. You are the Executive Vice President, Worldwide Client Relationships of Cerner and have been employed by Cerner since July 14, 2003. You have been the Executive Vice President, Worldwide Client Relationships of Cerner since October 1, 2017. B. You entered into an employment agreement with Cerner dated July 14, 2003 (your “Employment Agreement”) and a mutual arbitration agreement with Cerner dated November 23, 2015. C. You and Cerner wish to amend your Employment Agreement by adding contractual severance terms as set forth in this Executive Severance Agreement. D. In consideration for your continuing employment with Cerner, the restricted stock granted to you on December 14, 2017, the potential severance payments and potential acceleration of the vesting of outstanding equity incentive awards described herein, the potential benefits to you in the event of a Change in Control, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which you and Cerner hereby acknowledge, you and Cerner hereby agree to the following supplemental terms and conditions to your Employment Agreement. E. Definitions of capitalized terms used but not otherwise defined herein can be found in Appendix A. AGREEMENT In consideration of the mutual covenants, promises, and obligations set forth herein, the parties agree to amend and supplement your Employment Agreement as follows: 1. PARAGRAPH 2 MODIFICATION. Paragraph 2 of your Employment Agreement is deleted in its entirety and replaced with the following: 2. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONSHIP; SEVERANCE AND BENEFITS. To the extent permitted by law, your employment relationship with Cerner is “at will,” which means that you may resign from Cerner at any time, for any reason or for no reason at all, and without advance notice (except as described below). It also means that Cerner may terminate your employment at any time - for any legally permitted reason or for no reason at all and without advance notice, subject to Cerner’s potential obligations to you under Paragraph 2.C below. B. Resignation and Termination. 1. Termination by Cerner. Cerner may terminate your employment (i) at any time with or without Cause, or (ii) upon your Disability. Your employment with Cerner shall be deemed automatically terminated upon your death.

A reference to a party in this Separation Agreement necessarily includes those persons and/or entities described in the foregoing sentence. PREAMBLE WHEREAS, Waste Management, Inc. (together with any entity that is a direct or indirect majority-owned subsidiary of Waste Management, Inc., “Waste Management”) and Executive previously entered into that certain Employment Agreement dated September 2002, as may have been amended from time to time (the “Employment Agreement”); WHEREAS, pursuant to such Employment Agreement, Executive has been continuously employed by the Company or an affiliate thereof since such time; WHEREAS, the Parties agree that upon his separation, execution and non-revocation of a waiver and release of claims, Executive will receive certain benefits described in Exhibit C of this Separation Agreement; WHEREAS, the Company and Executive now jointly desire to enter into this Separation Agreement to set forth the terms and conditions of Executive’s termination; and NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the premises and agreements contained herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the Company and Executive hereby agree as follows: 1.Executive’s Separation. The employment of Executive with the Company shall terminate on August 11, 2018 (“Employment Termination Date”). The parties agree that, except as otherwise expressly specified herein, the Employment Agreement shall have been deemed terminated as of the Employment Termination Date. Executive acknowledges and agrees that all officer positions he held with any Waste Management entity ceased as of the Employment Termination Date. In addition, Executive shall resign from all industry or charitable board or trade group leadership positions he currently holds by virtue of his employment with the Company (including, without limitation, those positions set forth on Exhibit A). It is expressly agreed to and acknowledged by the parties that Executive is entitled to the compensation and benefits set forth in Exhibit B, (which, for the avoidance of doubt, shall survive termination of the Employment Agreement hereunder), whether or not he executes this Separation Agreement. 2.Payment of Additional Consideration. In consideration of the premises and promises herein contained, and subject to Executive executing and not revoking this Separation Agreement, it is agreed that the Company will provide Executive those certain benefits specifically detailed in Exhibit C to this Separation Agreement. It is expressly agreed to and acknowledged by the parties that Executive is not entitled to the benefits set forth in Exhibit C until such time as he executes this Separation Agreement and it becomes effective and irrevocable, by its terms. The consideration set forth in this Section 2 is in full, final and complete settlement of any and all claims which Executive could make in any complaint, charge, or civil action, whether for actual, nominal, compensatory, or punitive damages (including attorneys’ fees).

text: May 18, 2017 Re:Employment Agreement Dear Sheila: I have recently reviewed the terms and conditions of your employment and am pleased to provide this letter agreement (the “Agreement”) modifying the terms of your employment in your role as VP, Clinical Operations of Proteostasis Therapeutics, Inc. (“Proteostasis” or the “Company”). This Agreement amends, restates, and supersedes in its entirety the letter agreement dated September 29, 2016 between you and the Company (the “Offer Letter Agreement”). This Agreement is effective as of the date hereof. 1.This letter agreement outlines the terms of your employment with the Company. Position and Duties. You currently serve as VP, Clinical Operations of the Company. In this capacity, you will report directly to the Chief Development Officer (“CDO”) of the Company and will have responsibility for performing those duties as are customary for, and consistent with, your position with Company, as well as those duties as the CDO may from time to time designate. You shall use your best efforts and devote your full working time to performing your responsibilities for the Company. In addition, and as a condition of your continued employment with the Company, on or before you execute this Agreement, you shall execute the Company’s standard form of Employee Proprietary Information, Inventions, Non-Competition and Non-Solicitation Agreement (the “Restrictive Covenants Agreement”). Notwithstanding the foregoing, you may serve on other boards of directors or undertake other professional or charitable activities if (i) they do not conflict with any of your obligations to the Company and (ii) such activities are approved by the Board of Directors (the “Board”) or its Executive Committee. You may also undertake charitable activities without the approval of the Board or its Executive Committee if (i) they do not conflict with any of your obligations to the Company and (ii) they do not involve a material time commitment, individually or in the aggregate. 2.Compensation and Benefits. Your base salary will be at the rate of $300,150.00 per year and will be paid out on a bi-weekly basis for so long as you remain an employee of Proteostasis. Your base salary shall be reviewed annually by the Board or the Compensation Committee of the Board (the “Compensation Committee”). Proteostasis Therapeutics, Inc. | 200 Technology Square, Fourth Floor, Cambridge, MA 02139 | T: 617.225.0096 ACTIVE/86576310.2 Sheila Wilson May 18, 2017 (b)Bonus Plan. You will be eligible to receive an annual cash bonus pursuant to the Company’s Senior Executive Cash Incentive Bonus Plan, as it may be amended from time to time, with an individual “target bonus opportunity” for purposes of such plan of 30% of your base salary.

text: EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT AMENDED AND RESTATED EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT, dated as of November 25, 2019 (this “Employment Agreement”), by and between Evoqua Water Technologies LLC, a Delaware limited liability company (the “Company”), and Benedict J. Stas (the “Executive”) (each of the Executive and the Company, a “Party,” and collectively, the “Parties”) and, solely for purposes of Sections 2.5 and 3.2 hereof, Evoqua Water Technologies Corp. WHEREAS, the Parties previously entered into that certain Employment Agreement dated February 26, 2015 (the “Original Employment Agreement”), and amended on September 6, 2017 (the “First Amendment”) and June 22, 2018 (the “Second Amendment” and together with the Original Employment Agreement and the First Amendment, the “Prior Agreement”); WHEREAS, the Parties desire to amend and restate the Prior Agreement in its entirety as set forth herein; WHEREAS, the Company desires to continue to employ the Executive as Chief Financial Officer of Holdings and wishes to be assured of his services on the terms and conditions hereinafter set forth; and WHEREAS, the Executive desires to continue to be employed by the Company as Chief Financial Officer of Holdings and to perform and to serve the Company and Holdings on the terms and conditions hereinafter set forth. NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants contained herein and other valid consideration, the sufficiency of which is acknowledged, the Parties hereto agree as follows: Section 1.Employment. 1.1 Term. The term of the Executive’s employment under this Employment Agreement will commence on the date hereof and continue until terminated pursuant to Section 3 hereof (the period of the Executive’s employment hereunder, the “Employment Period”). During the Employment Period, the Executive shall serve as Chief Financial Officer and such other positions as an officer or director of Holdings and such affiliates of Holdings as Holdings shall determine from time to time, and shall report directly to the Chief Executive Officer of Holdings (the “Reporting Person”) or his designee. In his position of Chief Financial Officer of Holdings, the Executive shall perform duties assigned to him by the Reporting Person or his designee. During the Employment Period, the Executive shall devote substantially all of his business time and attention to the business and affairs of Holdings and the Company, shall faithfully serve Holdings and the Company, and shall conform to and comply with the lawful and reasonable directions and instructions given to him by the Reporting Person or his designee. During the Employment Period, the Executive shall use his best efforts to promote and serve the interests of Holdings and the Company and shall not engage in any other business activity, whether or not such activity shall be engaged in for pecuniary profit; provided, that the Executive may (a) serve any civic, charitable, educational or professional organization and (b) manage his personal investments, in each case so long as any such activities do not (x) violate the terms of this Employment Agreement (including Section 4) or (y) materially interfere with the Executive’s duties and responsibilities to Holdings or the Company. Section 2. Compensation.

text: EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT hwke_ex102.htm EXHIBIT 10.2 EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT This EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT (this “Agreement”), dated as of [__]1, 2019 (hereafter the “Effective Date”), is between Hawkeye Systems, Inc., a Nevada corporation (hereafter the “Company”) and Michael Mansouri (hereafter the “Executive“). The Company hereby employs the Executive, and the Executive hereby agrees to employment with the Company, upon all the terms and conditions set forth below. Executive represents and warrants that: (a) he has full power and authority to enter into this Agreement, (b) he is not restricted in any manner whatsoever from performing the duties described below, and (c) no agreement, covenant or other matter prohibits or limits her ability or authority to enter into this Agreement or perform all of the duties described below. The “Employment Term” and Executive’s employment under this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of three (3) years from the date of this Agreement, ending at the close of business on September __, 2020, provided, however, that the Employment Term shall automatically extend for successive one-year periods (such extensions also being referred to as the “Employment Term”), as long as neither party has given written notice to the other party at least 30 days prior to the end of the then current term that such term shall not be extended, and further provided that the Agreement has not been terminated earlier in accordance with the provisions set forth below. text: 1. **Analyze the Request:** * **Topic:** Opinion legal issues concerning the subject matter (broad, implies writing an opinion passage on a legal issue, perhaps a specific but unstated subject matter, so I need to choose a plausible legal subject or keep it generalized but substantive). Let's frame it as a legal opinion on the tortious interference or intellectual property issues surrounding the unauthorized use of proprietary algorithms in emerging tech (a common "subject matter" for complex legal opinions). * **Word Count:** Approximately 495 words. * **Format:** Clean prose only. NO markdown, NO preamble, NO title, NO commentary, NO explanation. * **Tone/Register:** Substantive legal document, not a generic essay. Read like a formal legal opinion (memorandum of law, judicial opinion, or advisory brief). 2. **Drafting - Strategy & Structure:** * *Introduction:* State the legal issue at hand clearly. E.g., The unauthorized extraction and deployment of proprietary algorithmic architectures raises substantial questions under both trade secret and copyright frameworks. * *Body Paragraph 1 (Trade Secret):* Discuss the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) implications. Elements of misappropriation, economic value from secrecy, reasonable measures. * *Body Paragraph 2 (Copyright/Intellectual Property):* Discuss the idea/expression dichotomy. Algorithms as processes (unprotectable) vs. specific code (protectable). Merger doctrine. * *Body Paragraph 3 (Tortious Interference):* Interference with prospective economic advantage or contractual relations. * *Conclusion:* Synthesize the opinion, stating the likely judicial outcome or the strength of the claims. 3. **Drafting - Execution (incorporating legal terminology):** The extraction and subsequent deployment of proprietary algorithmic architectures by competing enterprises present multifaceted legal challenges that straddle the demarcations between trade secret misappropriation, copyright infringement, and tortious interference with contractual relations. At the nucleus of this dispute is the question of whether the subject matter—a predictive analytics engine leveraging machine learning—qualifies for statutory protection under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, or whether its functional imperatives render it unprotectable as a matter of law. To assert a viable claim for trade secret misappropriation, the plaintiff must demonstrate that the algorithm derives independent economic value from not being generally known to, or readily ascertainable by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use, and that reasonable efforts were maintained to preserve its secrecy. While the underlying mathematical principles of the algorithm are undoubtedly within the public domain, the specific weighting of variables and the unique training methodology employed constitute protectable elements, provided adequate confidentiality protocols were enforced. Failure to implement robust access restrictions or nondisclosure agreements may fatally undermine this claim, as courts consistently hold that information readily discernible through reverse engineering lacks the requisite secrecy. Concurrently, copyright claims concerning the subject matter face formidable barriers rooted in the idea-expression dichotomy and the merger doctrine. Although the literal code embodying the algorithm is presumptively protectable under the Copyright Act, the functional logic and organizational structure of the software often transcend protectable expression and merge with the unprotectable idea itself.

label: ai
text: This opinion addresses the legal issues raised by the proposed assignment and commercial use of the subject matter. I conclude that the client may proceed if it secures a written transfer from each current owner, confirms the absence of superior third-party rights, and limits use to the scope described in the transaction documents. Ownership presents the first legal issue. The record must identify who created the subject matter, who paid for it, and whether any prior agreement transferred rights. Payment alone does not establish ownership. If an independent contractor created the work, the client needs an assignment signed by that contractor unless a valid work-made-for-hire rule applies. If an employee created it within the scope of employment, the employer owns the relevant rights, subject to any contrary agreement. The file should include employment records, contractor agreements, invoices, delivery materials, and correspondence showing the intended allocation of rights. The proposed use also raises infringement risk. A seller cannot transfer more than it owns. If the subject matter incorporates licensed material, open-source code, stock content, personal data, or third-party trademarks, the client must review those source rights before relying on the seller’s warranty. A broad warranty helps allocate loss between the parties, but it does not prevent a third-party claim. The agreement should require disclosure of embedded third-party material and should give the client a right to reject or remove disputed elements before closing. Regulatory compliance requires separate attention. If the subject matter includes personal information, the client must identify the categories of data, the source of collection, the purpose of processing, and the jurisdictions involved. Consent, notice, retention, and security duties may limit transfer or downstream use. A contract clause stating that the data may be transferred will not cure a statutory defect in the original collection. The client should require evidence that the seller collected and maintained the information under applicable privacy law. The agreement should also address remedies with precision. The indemnity should cover infringement claims, privacy claims arising from pre-closing conduct, and losses caused by inaccurate ownership representations. The defense clause should allow the client to control any matter that affects its business operations or ongoing use. Liability caps should exclude intentional misconduct and undisclosed third-party rights, because those risks defeat the core purpose of the transaction. On the present record, the transaction is legally supportable but incomplete. The client should not close until the seller delivers executed assignments, a schedule of third-party components, proof of authority to transfer the subject matter, and compliance materials for any personal data.

label: ai
text: The legal issues raised by this subject matter fall into three categories, each of which the court must resolve before reaching the merits. The threshold question is one of statutory interpretation. Here the parties dispute whether the operative provision reaches conduct that occurred before the amendment took effect. Its text supplies the answer. Section 4(b) applies to any transaction "completed on or after" the effective date, and the transaction here closed two weeks earlier. Retroactive application would require language the legislature did not use. The second issue concerns standing. According to the defendant, the plaintiff suffered no concrete injury because the alleged breach caused no measurable loss. That argument misreads the record. The plaintiff paid a premium of forty thousand dollars in reliance on the representation now shown to be false. A financial outlay traceable to the challenged conduct establishes injury in fact. The defendant's contrary reading would deny standing to any party who cannot yet quantify consequential damages, a rule no authority supports. The third issue is the scope of the remedy. Even accepting liability, the defendant contends that rescission is unavailable because the plaintiff continued to accept benefits under the contract. The doctrine of election has force only where the party seeking rescission had full knowledge of the facts at the time it accepted those benefits. Here, the plaintiff learned of the misrepresentation months after the last payment. Continued performance under a contract believed valid does not waive the right to unwind it once the fraud surfaces. These questions resolve in the plaintiff's favor, but the analysis carries a limit worth stating. Nothing here decides whether punitive relief is warranted. That determination depends on the defendant's state of mind, a matter the present record leaves open. The court holds only that the statute governs, the plaintiff has standing, and rescission remains available. The remaining questions of intent and aggravated damages await further proceedings, where the factual dispute over the defendant's knowledge must be tried.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: It is my opinion that the threshold legal issue presented by the record is whether the tribunal possesses subject matter jurisdiction over the controversy as pleaded, because no act of consent, waiver, stipulation, or procedural default can confer authority where the law has withheld it. Subject matter jurisdiction is not merely a technical pleading requirement; it is the legal foundation upon which the court’s power to adjudicate rests. Accordingly, the issue may be raised by any party at any stage of the proceeding and, where apparent, must be addressed by the court sua sponte before reaching the merits. The pleadings must be examined not for the strength of the claimant’s proof, but for whether the asserted claim falls within the class of disputes the tribunal is authorized to hear. A complaint may be deficient, doubtful, or ultimately unsuccessful and yet still invoke jurisdiction if it alleges a cognizable claim within the statutory or constitutional grant of authority. Conversely, a well-pleaded factual narrative cannot sustain adjudication if the dispute is assigned exclusively to another forum, is barred by sovereign immunity, is nonjusticiable, or lacks the jurisdictional amount, status, or statutory predicate required by law. Where jurisdiction depends upon a statutory cause of action, the court should distinguish between a failure to state a claim and a true jurisdictional defect. Modern authority generally cautions against treating every statutory limitation as jurisdictional unless the legislature has clearly indicated that the limitation speaks to the adjudicatory power of the court rather than to the elements of liability. This distinction is consequential. If the defect is jurisdictional, dismissal is ordinarily without prejudice and the judgment is vulnerable to collateral attack. If the defect concerns only the sufficiency of the claim, the matter may be resolved on the merits and may carry preclusive effect. The same analysis applies to administrative exhaustion and exclusive-remedy provisions. If the governing statute makes exhaustion a mandatory condition to the agency’s or court’s authority, noncompliance deprives the tribunal of power to proceed. If, however, exhaustion is a claims-processing rule, it may be forfeited if not timely asserted. The language, structure, and purpose of the enactment are therefore controlling. Courts should avoid jurisdictional characterizations based solely on labels or historical practice where the legislature has not clearly imposed such a consequence. On the present assumptions, the proper disposition would be to require the party invoking jurisdiction to establish the jurisdictional facts by competent allegations and, if contested, by evidence sufficient to satisfy the applicable burden. Any factual disputes bearing solely on jurisdiction may be resolved by the court without submitting the merits to trial, provided the parties are afforded a fair opportunity to be heard. If the jurisdictional basis cannot be established, dismissal is required notwithstanding considerations of efficiency or perceived equity.

text: The legal issues presented turn on whether the subject matter falls within the statute's protective scope. We hold that it does not. The text limits coverage to transactions completed before the effective date, and the transaction here closed two weeks after that date. The plaintiff's contrary reading would require us to treat the deadline as advisory, but the statute uses mandatory language and provides no exception. The plaintiff argues that the purpose of the statute supports a broader reading. Purpose cannot override text that admits of one meaning. The words "completed on or before" set a firm cutoff, and no ambiguity invites resort to legislative history. Even if we consulted that history, it confirms the plain reading. The sponsors described the deadline as a bright line meant to give parties certainty about their obligations. A second issue concerns the remedy the plaintiff seeks. The complaint requests rescission and damages. These remedies rest on the same theory of coverage, so both fail once coverage is denied. The plaintiff offers no independent basis for either. Because the claim depends entirely on the statute, its rejection ends the case. The defendant raises a procedural objection that we need not resolve. The defendant contends the plaintiff waived the coverage argument by failing to raise it below. We decide the matter on the merits and leave the waiver question open. Nothing in this opinion should be read to endorse or reject the defendant's procedural theory. We recognize that this result leaves the plaintiff without a remedy under the statute. That consequence follows from the choice the legislature made. Courts apply the line the legislature drew rather than the line a party wishes it had drawn. If the deadline produces harsh outcomes in close cases, the remedy lies with the legislature. The judgment of the trial court is affirmed. The plaintiff's transaction fell outside the statute, the requested remedies depend on coverage that does not exist, and the procedural objection remains for another day. Costs are taxed to the plaintiff.

text: The legal issues presented by the subject matter warrant careful examination, for they touch upon principles that resist easy or mechanical resolution. At the outset, it must be acknowledged that the questions raised do not admit of a single dispositive answer, but instead require the balancing of competing considerations, each grounded in established doctrine and each deserving of measured weight. The threshold inquiry concerns the proper characterization of the matter before us. It is a settled principle that the substance of a claim, rather than the label affixed to it by the parties, governs the analysis a court must undertake. Where the pleadings and the factual record diverge, the reviewing tribunal is obliged to look past nomenclature and ascertain the true nature of the rights and obligations asserted. This approach ensures that the resolution reached rests upon a sound understanding of the underlying legal relationship, rather than upon formalistic distinctions that would frustrate the ends of justice. Turning to the merits, the controlling authorities establish that the rights in question are neither absolute nor without limitation. The jurisprudence in this area reflects a consistent recognition that individual entitlements must be reconciled with legitimate countervailing interests, whether those interests be public or private in character. Accordingly, any determination must proceed from a candid assessment of the burdens imposed and the benefits secured, weighed against the foreseeable consequences of the interpretation advanced. It bears emphasis that the doctrine of stare decisis, while not an inexorable command, counsels strongly in favor of adherence to settled precedent absent compelling justification for departure. The stability and predictability of the law depend upon this fidelity, and litigants are entitled to order their affairs in reliance upon principles that have been consistently applied. A court should therefore be reluctant to disturb established rules unless the reasoning underlying them has been demonstrably eroded by subsequent developments or exposed as fundamentally unsound. Nor can the analysis proceed without attention to considerations of fairness and equity, which have long animated the development of the law in this field. The equitable maxims, though not to be invoked as a substitute for legal reasoning, serve to temper the rigor of doctrine where its rigid application would work manifest injustice. The tribunal must remain mindful that the ultimate objective is the just resolution of the controversy, informed by both the letter and the spirit of the governing principles. In sum, the legal issues concerning the subject matter demand a disciplined and comprehensive approach, one that honors precedent, respects the balance of competing interests, and remains faithful to the enduring purposes that the law is designed to serve.

text: The question is whether this court may exercise subject matter jurisdiction over a dispute arising from a private services agreement that refers to federal standards but pleads claims for breach of contract, negligent misrepresentation, and restitution under state law. I conclude that federal question jurisdiction does not exist on the present pleadings, and diversity jurisdiction depends on facts that the removing party has not established. A federal issue does not appear on the face of the complaint. The plaintiff seeks payment for work performed, damages for alleged misstatements during contract formation, and return of funds retained after termination. Those claims arise under state law. The agreement incorporates federal regulatory language, but incorporation does not convert a contract claim into one arising under federal law. Parties may borrow federal standards as contractual measures of performance. When they do so, the court enforces the bargain unless Congress has created a federal cause of action or the plaintiff’s right to relief requires resolution of a substantial federal issue. This record does not meet that standard. The complaint can succeed or fail based on ordinary questions of contract interpretation, reliance, causation, and damages. The plaintiff need not prove that the defendant violated a federal statute as an element of any claim. At most, federal regulations may inform whether the defendant performed with the degree of care promised in the agreement. That use of federal law is evidentiary. It does not create federal jurisdiction. The defendant argues that uniform federal interpretation is necessary because the agreement concerns a regulated industry. That position reaches too far. Many contracts operate against a federal regulatory background. If that background alone supplied jurisdiction, federal courts would absorb broad categories of commercial disputes that state courts routinely decide. The jurisdictional inquiry turns on the claims pleaded, not on the regulated character of the business. Congress has not shown an intent to displace state contract remedies in this setting. Preemption also does not support jurisdiction. Ordinary preemption offers a defense, and a defense cannot create federal question jurisdiction. Complete preemption applies only when federal law so occupies a field that it replaces the state claim with a federal one. The defendant identifies no statute with that effect. The possibility that federal law may limit available remedies or shape the standard of performance can be addressed by the state court if the case returns there. Diversity jurisdiction requires complete diversity and an amount in controversy exceeding the statutory threshold. The notice of removal alleges the parties’ residence, not their citizenship. Residence does not establish domicile for individuals, and it does not identify the citizenship of limited liability companies or partnerships. The removing party bears that burden. The amount in controversy allegation also rests on projected consequential damages without a factual basis in the complaint or attached records. Because federal courts possess limited jurisdiction, the court must resolve jurisdiction before reaching the merits.

I'll write it as an opinion piece analyzing the evolving jurisprudence around subject matter jurisdiction and subject matter specificity in commercial contracts. * Key points to cover: The threshold issue of subject matter jurisdiction (cannot be waived, must be proven), the contractual issue of ambiguous subject matter descriptions (parol evidence rule, integration clauses), and the policy considerations underlying strict scrutiny of subject matter definitions. 3. **Drafting - Section by Section (aiming for ~481 words):** * *Introduction:* The concept of "subject matter" serves as the foundational pillar upon which both jurisdictional authority and contractual enforceability rest. Yet, the adjudication of subject matter issues remains among the most fraught arenas in contemporary jurisprudence. * *Jurisdictional context:* At the jurisdictional threshold, subject matter jurisdiction cannot be created by consent, waiver, or estoppel. Courts consistently hold that the statutory grant of authority defines the boundaries of adjudicatory power. When a plaintiff invokes a tribunal's authority, the precise delineation of the subject matter becomes a non-negotiable prerequisite. A tribunal that oversteps its subject matter limits renders a void judgment, irrespective of the equities at play. The rigid adherence to this doctrine, while preserving constitutional and statutory boundaries, often leads to harsh procedural outcomes, particularly where litigants have expended substantial resources litigating the merits only to discover a latent jurisdictional defect in the subject matter. * *Contractual context:* Beyond the jurisdictional sphere, subject matter ambiguity in commercial agreements presents equally intractable dilemmas. The subject matter of a contract must be identified with sufficient specificity to enforce the parties' mutual obligations. When the subject matter is vague or illusory, courts are forced to navigate the tension between the parol evidence rule and the objective theory of contracts. Extrinsic evidence routinely becomes necessary to ascertain the precise subject matter contemplated by the drafting parties, yet its admission threatens to undermine the certainty of integrated agreements. This tension has precipitated a circuit split regarding the threshold specificity required to survive a motion to dismiss for failure to adequately define the subject matter.

text: The threshold issue is subject matter jurisdiction, and the record does not permit the court to bypass it. A court may decide only the class of dispute that the constitution and statutes assign to it. Consent, waiver, convenience, and litigation conduct cannot create that authority. When the pleadings reveal a jurisdictional defect, the court must address it before reaching breach, damages, or equitable relief. The complaint alleges a contractual dispute between private parties. It invokes federal jurisdiction on the ground that the agreement required performance in interstate commerce and refers to federal regulations governing the industry. Those allegations do not create a federal question. A claim arises under federal law when federal law supplies the cause of action or when the plaintiff’s right to relief depends on the resolution of a disputed and substantial federal issue. The complaint seeks payment under a private contract. State law supplies the cause of action. The federal regulations may inform the standard of performance, but they do not transform the claim into one arising under federal law. The plaintiff also relies on a forum selection clause naming this court as the chosen forum. That clause cannot confer subject matter jurisdiction. It may affect venue if an independent jurisdictional basis exists. It may also support transfer or dismissal in favor of a proper forum. It does not expand the statutory grant of power to federal courts. Parties may choose where to litigate among courts that have authority. They may not assign authority to a court that lacks it. Diversity jurisdiction fails for a separate reason. The pleadings identify the plaintiff as a limited liability company but allege only its state of organization and principal office. For diversity purposes, an unincorporated entity takes the citizenship of each of its members. The complaint does not identify those members or their citizenship. The court therefore cannot determine whether complete diversity exists. The amount in controversy exceeds the statutory minimum, but amount alone cannot cure incomplete or unproven citizenship. The defendant asks the court to dismiss the case with prejudice because the plaintiff filed in the wrong court after receiving notice of the jurisdictional defect. That remedy exceeds the proper consequence of a jurisdictional dismissal. A dismissal for lack of subject matter jurisdiction does not adjudicate the merits. It prevents this court from deciding the merits. The plaintiff remains subject to any limitations defense in a competent court, but this court cannot impose claim preclusion through a merits dismissal when it lacks power to decide the claim. The court should grant the motion to dismiss without prejudice. The plaintiff may file in state court or amend in federal court if it can allege complete diversity in good faith. Any amendment must identify each member of the limited liability company and the citizenship of each member. If any member shares citizenship with the defendant, diversity jurisdiction remains absent.

text: The legal issues raised by this subject matter turn on questions of jurisdiction, statutory interpretation, and the allocation of burdens between the parties. Each question demands separate treatment, and none resolves the others by implication. Jurisdiction comes first because the court cannot reach the merits without it. Under the statute, authority arises only where the disputed transaction touches a protected interest, and the record must establish that connection before any further analysis proceeds. Where the pleadings fail to allege facts supporting jurisdiction, dismissal follows without regard to the strength of the underlying claim. A party invoking the court's power bears the burden of demonstrating it, and conclusory allegations do not satisfy that requirement. The interpretive question is closer. The governing text uses terms that carry both a technical meaning and an ordinary one, and the two readings produce different outcomes. When the statutory language admits of a single reasonable construction, the analysis ends with the text. Here it does not. The provision defining eligibility conflicts with the provision limiting recovery, and the two cannot both operate at full scope. A court confronting such a conflict must give effect to the more specific provision, which controls over the general one addressing the same subject. That canon resolves the tension in favor of the narrower reading. Legislative history offers limited assistance. The committee reports address an earlier version of the bill, and the operative language was amended after those reports issued. Reliance on the reports would attribute to the enacted text an intent the drafters abandoned. The court should therefore rest its construction on the statutory language as adopted, informed by the structure of the surrounding provisions. Turning to the burdens of proof, the allocation follows the substantive framework the statute establishes. The claimant must prove the elements of the violation by a preponderance of the evidence. Once that showing is made, the defendant may raise an affirmative defense, and the burden of establishing that defense rests with the defendant. This allocation reflects the ordinary rule that the party asserting a proposition must prove it. Nothing in the statute displaces that rule, and the parties have identified no authority requiring a different distribution. Two subsidiary points warrant attention. First, the standard of review governs the weight this court gives the findings below. Factual findings receive deference and stand unless clearly erroneous, while legal conclusions receive fresh review. The interpretive dispute is a legal one, so the trial court's reading commands no deference. Second, the remedy question remains open. Even if liability attaches, the scope of relief depends on findings the record does not yet contain, and remand may be necessary to develop them. These issues, taken together, define the boundaries of the dispute. Resolution of the jurisdictional and interpretive questions determines whether the merits reach the court at all, and the burden allocation shapes how those merits are decided if they do.

text: Based upon the facts presented and the documents reviewed, the principal legal issues concerning the subject matter are enforceability, allocation of risk, regulatory compliance, and the availability of remedies in the event of breach or interference. Any legal conclusion must begin with the character of the underlying transaction or relationship, because the governing rules may differ materially depending on whether the subject matter is treated as a contractual right, a property interest, a statutory entitlement, a regulated activity, or an intangible commercial asset. The parties’ labels are relevant but not controlling; a court will examine the substance of the arrangement, the conduct of the parties, and the practical consequences of enforcement. If the subject matter arises from an agreement, the first inquiry is whether a valid and binding contract exists. There must be competent parties, mutual assent, consideration, sufficiently definite terms, and no applicable defense to formation. Ambiguities in material terms may not necessarily defeat enforcement, but they may affect the scope of obligations and the measure of damages. Where the agreement contains integration, limitation-of-liability, indemnity, forum-selection, arbitration, confidentiality, or termination provisions, those clauses should be examined closely, as they may substantially alter the parties’ rights even where liability is otherwise established. Ownership and authority present separate concerns. A party cannot convey, license, encumber, or restrict rights it does not possess. Accordingly, any representation concerning control of the subject matter should be supported by documentary evidence, course-of-dealing evidence, or applicable legal presumption. If third-party rights exist, including liens, licenses, security interests, easements, intellectual property claims, privacy interests, or statutory use restrictions, those interests may limit the ability to exploit or transfer the subject matter and may expose the acting party to claims for infringement, conversion, misappropriation, breach of confidence, or tortious interference. Compliance issues are also significant. Even a private arrangement may be unenforceable, voidable, or subject to penalties if its performance would violate statute, public policy, licensing requirements, consumer protection rules, employment laws, data protection obligations, securities regulations, competition laws, or industry-specific standards. The existence of a regulatory scheme does not automatically create a private cause of action, but violation of such a scheme may support negligence per se, unfair trade practice claims, rescission, administrative enforcement, or evidentiary presumptions adverse to the noncompliant party. The available remedies depend upon the nature of the right affected and the adequacy of monetary relief. Damages may include direct losses, consequential damages if foreseeable and not contractually excluded, restitution, disgorgement, statutory damages, attorneys’ fees where authorized, and pre- or post-judgment interest. Equitable relief, including injunction, specific performance, declaratory judgment, or constructive trust, may be appropriate where the subject matter is unique, confidential, time-sensitive, or not readily compensable by money damages. In my opinion, the legal position is strongest where the governing documents clearly define the subject matter, identify the parties with authority over it, allocate known risks, preserve necessary evidence, and comply with applicable law.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

text: The legal issues presented by this subject matter arise from the intersection of contract interpretation and statutory obligation. Two questions control the analysis. First, whether the parties intended the disputed term to bind future assignees. Second, whether the governing statute displaces that private allocation of risk. The record answers both. On the first question, the operative language appears in section 6.2 of the agreement. That provision extends all obligations to successors and assigns without reservation. The defendant urges a narrower reading, but nothing in the surrounding text supports a carve-out for assignees who acquire the property after default. Where drafters intended limits, they said so, as the express exclusion in section 6.4 demonstrates. The absence of a parallel limit in 6.2 reflects a deliberate choice, and the court reads the contract as written. The defendant next argues that the term is ambiguous. It is not. Ambiguity requires two reasonable readings of the same language. The defendant offers only one reading grounded in the text and a second grounded in commercial preference. Preference is not construction. A party's wish for a different bargain does not create doubt about the bargain it struck. The court declines to manufacture ambiguity to rescue a sophisticated party from a term it accepted. The statutory question is closer but resolves against displacement. Section 12 of the enabling act preserves private agreements except where they conflict with a mandatory duty. The duty invoked here concerns notice to junior lienholders. The agreement does not waive that duty. It allocates the cost of compliance. A cost allocation and a waiver are different things, and only the latter would trigger the statute's override. Because the parties allocated rather than waived, the statute leaves their arrangement intact. The defendant's reliance on Harmon is misplaced. There, the agreement purported to eliminate the notice requirement entirely, and the court struck the provision as contrary to the statute's protective purpose. The provision here does the opposite. It assumes the requirement and assigns responsibility for meeting it. Harmon condemns evasion of a duty. It does not condemn contracting about who bears the burden of performing one. A final issue concerns remedy. The plaintiff seeks specific performance rather than damages. Specific performance is available where the subject matter is unique and damages are inadequate. The interest at stake is a right of first refusal over a single commercial parcel with no market substitute. Damages cannot restore that interest, because no sum reproduces the opportunity the defendant's breach destroyed. The equitable remedy fits the injury. The court holds that the successor obligation binds the defendant, that the term is unambiguous, that the statute does not displace the private allocation, and that specific performance is the appropriate remedy. This conclusion follows from the text of the agreement and the structure of the statute, read together as the parties and the legislature framed them. The defendant's contrary position rests on preference, not on the governing sources.

text: The legal issues presented by the subject matter require careful examination of both the governing statutory framework and the body of precedent that has developed around it. At the threshold, the question of jurisdiction must be resolved, for no substantive determination may proceed absent a proper foundation establishing that the forum possesses both subject-matter and personal jurisdiction over the controversy and the parties before it. Where the pleadings fail to establish these prerequisites, dismissal is warranted irrespective of the apparent merits of the claims asserted. Assuming jurisdiction is properly invoked, the analysis turns to the applicable standard of review. It is well settled that questions of law are reviewed de novo, affording no deference to the conclusions reached below, while findings of fact are disturbed only upon a showing of clear error. This distinction bears directly upon the disposition of the issues raised, as the characterization of a given question as one of law or fact frequently proves dispositive of the outcome. The party seeking reversal bears the burden of demonstrating that the challenged ruling falls outside the permissible range of discretion afforded to the tribunal of first instance. Turning to the substance, the controlling authority establishes a multi-factor inquiry that must be applied to the facts as developed in the record. No single factor is dispositive; rather, the tribunal must weigh the totality of the circumstances, giving appropriate consideration to the purposes underlying the governing rule. In this respect, it is instructive to recall that statutory provisions are to be construed in accordance with their plain meaning, and that resort to extrinsic aids of interpretation is appropriate only where the text is genuinely ambiguous. The legislative history, where consulted, confirms rather than contradicts the interpretation compelled by the ordinary meaning of the enacted language. The countervailing considerations advanced by the opposing party, though not without force, do not withstand scrutiny. The contention that a contrary rule better serves the interests of judicial economy misapprehends the nature of the inquiry, which is directed not to administrative convenience but to the vindication of the substantive rights the statute was designed to protect. Nor does the asserted reliance interest justify departure from the established rule, for the party invoking that interest was on constructive notice of the governing standard at all relevant times. In light of the foregoing, the proper resolution of the issues presented follows from a faithful application of settled principles to the facts of record. The conclusion reached is neither novel nor an extension of existing doctrine, but rather a straightforward application of principles long recognized in this jurisdiction. Any residual concerns regarding the equities are more appropriately addressed to the legislature than to a court constrained by the text before it.

text: The appeal concerns subject-matter jurisdiction, not the ultimate strength of the plaintiff’s statutory claim. After determining that the dispute belonged before the state data protection agency in the first instance, the trial court dismissed the complaint. That ruling misapplied the statute. The agency may investigate violations, impose civil penalties, and issue compliance orders, but the legislature also created a private cause of action for consumers whose personal information is wrongfully disclosed. When a statute gives a court authority to award damages for a defined injury, an administrative enforcement scheme does not displace that authority unless the statute says so. The complaint alleges a concrete injury. According to the plaintiff, the defendant collected biometric identifiers, failed to provide the written notice required by statute, and retained the identifiers after the stated purpose for collection had ended. The statute treats that conduct as an invasion of a personal privacy interest, not as a procedural defect detached from harm. Legislatures may define legally protected interests that did not exist at common law, and courts must respect that definition when the asserted injury bears a close relationship to interests traditionally protected by privacy law. Control over biometric data has that relationship. A fingerprint or facial scan cannot be replaced like a password. Once disclosed, it creates a continuing risk of misuse that the individual cannot fully erase. The defendant argues that the plaintiff lacks standing because he does not allege identity theft, financial loss, or public dissemination of his data. That argument narrows the statute beyond its text. The Act protects informed consent before collection and timely deletion after use. A violation occurs when a covered entity takes or keeps biometric data without satisfying those duties. Requiring proof of downstream financial harm would convert a consent statute into a compensation statute for identity theft. No court has authority to rewrite the claim in that manner. The exhaustion doctrine does not require dismissal. Exhaustion applies when the legislature gives an agency exclusive initial authority or when judicial review depends on a completed administrative record. This statute does neither. Instead, it authorizes agency enforcement and private litigation in separate provisions. The remedies also differ. Through regulatory orders, the agency protects the public, while an injured consumer seeks statutory damages and injunctive relief tailored to the defendant’s conduct. Concurrent authority may create overlapping proceedings, but overlap does not defeat jurisdiction. Nor does the primary jurisdiction doctrine support dismissal. Courts invoke that doctrine when an issue demands technical agency expertise or uniform regulatory judgment. The issues presented here are legal and factual questions that courts decide in ordinary litigation. Whether the defendant collected biometric identifiers, provided the required notice, obtained written consent, and retained the data longer than the statute permits are questions a court can resolve without prior agency action. Those questions do not require the agency to set policy before a court can act.

At the forefront of this analysis is the fundamental question of subject matter jurisdiction, a threshold requirement that cannot be waived by party consent or forfeiture. The court possesses an independent obligation to ensure that its authority to adjudicate the specific contours of the subject matter is firmly anchored in constitutional and statutory grants of power. Where the subject matter implicates specialized regulatory frameworks, the risk of preemptive displacement looms large. Consequently, any opinion rendered must rigorously examine whether the subject matter falls within the preempted field, thereby stripping the forum of its competence to entertain the dispute. Furthermore, even where jurisdictional competency exists, the substantive legal issues concerning the subject matter demand a meticulous choice-of-law analysis.
